randomNumber:
# Generate a random integer using Get-Random
# Default range is 0 to Int32.MaxValue (2,147,483,647)
$randomInt = Get-Random
Write-Host "Random Integer (full range): $randomInt"

# Generate a random integer within a specific range (e.g., 1 to 100)
# Minimum is inclusive, Maximum is exclusive by default for integers
$minValue = 1
$maxValueExclusive = 101 # To include 100, max must be 101
$randomInRange = Get-Random -Minimum $minValue -Maximum $maxValueExclusive
Write-Host "Random Integer (${minValue}-$($maxValueExclusive - 1)): $randomInRange" # Use $() for expression in string

# Generate a random floating-point number between 0.0 and 1.0
# Requires specifying -Minimum 0.0 -Maximum 1.0
$randomFloat = Get-Random -Minimum 0.0 -Maximum 1.0
Write-Host "Random Float (0.0 <= x < 1.0): $randomFloat"

# Select a random item from a collection
$myArray = "apple", "banana", "cherry", "date"
$randomFruit = Get-Random -InputObject $myArray
Write-Host "Random fruit from list: $randomFruit"
reverseString:
# Method 1: Using .NET char array conversion and reverse
$originalString = "PowerShell"
$charArray = $originalString.ToCharArray()
[array]::Reverse($charArray)
$reversedStringNet = -join $charArray # Join characters back into a string

Write-Host "Original: $originalString"
Write-Host "Reversed (.NET): $reversedStringNet"

# Method 2: Loop through characters (less idiomatic but works)
$reversedStringLoop = '' # Initialize empty string
# Iterate backwards using indices
for ($i = $originalString.Length - 1; $i -ge 0; $i--) {
    $reversedStringLoop += $originalString[$i]
}
Write-Host "Reversed (Loop): $reversedStringLoop"

# Method 3: PowerShell 7+ shortcut (negative index range) - More concise
# Requires PowerShell 7.0 or later
# $reversedStringPS7 = $originalString[-1..-$originalString.Length] -join ''
# Write-Host "Reversed (PS7+): $reversedStringPS7"
fibonacci:
# Function to generate the first N Fibonacci numbers iteratively
function Get-FibonacciSequence {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory=$true)]
        [ValidateRange(1, [int]::MaxValue)] # Basic validation
        [int]$Count
    )

    $sequence = @() # Initialize an empty array
    $a = [int64]0 # Use 64-bit integers to avoid overflow for a while
    $b = [int64]1

    for ($i = 0; $i -lt $Count; $i++) {
        $sequence += $a
        $next = $a + $b
        # Check for potential overflow before assignment (basic check)
        if ($next -lt $a -or $next -lt $b) {
            Write-Warning "Overflow detected generating Fibonacci sequence near term $($i + 1)."
            break
        }
        $a = $b
        $b = $next
    }
    return $sequence
}

# Example: Get the first 10 Fibonacci numbers
$fib10 = Get-FibonacciSequence -Count 10
Write-Host "First 10 Fibonacci numbers:"
Write-Host ($fib10 -join ', ') # Join array elements for printing

# Example: Get the first 15 numbers
Write-Host "First 15 Fibonacci numbers:"
Get-FibonacciSequence -Count 15 | Out-String # Another way to display

# Example: Large count (might overflow standard int64 around 93rd term)
# Get-FibonacciSequence -Count 95
factorial:
# Function to calculate factorial iteratively
function Get-Factorial {
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory=$true)]
        [ValidateRange(0, 20)] # Factorial of 21+ overflows standard int64
        [int]$Number
    )

    if ($Number -lt 0) {
        Write-Error "Factorial is not defined for negative numbers."
        return # Or throw
    }

    $result = [int64]1 # Use 64-bit integer
    if ($Number -eq 0) {
        return $result
    }

    for ($i = 1; $i -le $Number; $i++) {
        # Basic overflow check before multiplication
        if ($result -gt ([int64]::MaxValue / $i)) {
             Write-Error "Overflow detected calculating factorial for ${Number}."
             return
        }
        $result *= $i
    }
    return $result
}

# Example usage
$num = 5
$fact = Get-Factorial -Number $num
Write-Host "Factorial of $num is: $fact"

$num = 0
$fact = Get-Factorial -Number $num
Write-Host "Factorial of $num is: $fact"

$num = 20 # Max for standard Int64
$fact = Get-Factorial -Number $num
Write-Host "Factorial of $num is: $fact"

# Example of number too large (will hit ValidateRange or overflow check)
# Get-Factorial -Number 21
# Get-Factorial -Number -3 # Negative number example
fileExists:
$fileToCheck = "powershell_exists_test.ps1" # Example file
$dirToCheck = "MyPoshFolder"             # Example directory
$nonExistent = "NoSuchThingHere"

# Create dummy file/dir
New-Item -ItemType File -Path $fileToCheck -Force | Out-Null
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $dirToCheck -Force | Out-Null
Write-Host "Created dummy file '$fileToCheck' and directory '$dirToCheck'"

Write-Host "--- Checking Existence (Any Type) using Test-Path ---"
Write-Host "'$fileToCheck' exists? $(Test-Path -Path $fileToCheck)"
Write-Host "'$dirToCheck' exists? $(Test-Path -Path $dirToCheck)"
Write-Host "'$nonExistent' exists? $(Test-Path -Path $nonExistent)"

Write-Host "--- Checking Specific Type using Test-Path -PathType ---"
Write-Host "'$fileToCheck' is a File (Leaf)? $(Test-Path -Path $fileToCheck -PathType Leaf)"
Write-Host "'$dirToCheck' is a File (Leaf)? $(Test-Path -Path $dirToCheck -PathType Leaf)" # Should be false

Write-Host "'$fileToCheck' is a Directory (Container)? $(Test-Path -Path $fileToCheck -PathType Container)" # Should be false
Write-Host "'$dirToCheck' is a Directory (Container)? $(Test-Path -Path $dirToCheck -PathType Container)"

# Alternative: Using Get-Item / Get-ChildItem and checking properties (more object-oriented)
# try {
#     $item = Get-Item -Path $fileToCheck -ErrorAction Stop
#     Write-Host "'$fileToCheck' exists (Get-Item). PSIsContainer: $($item.PSIsContainer)" # False for files
# } catch { Write-Host "'$fileToCheck' does not exist (Get-Item)." }

# Clean up
Remove-Item -Path $fileToCheck -Force
Remove-Item -Path $dirToCheck -Force -Recurse
Write-Host "Cleaned up dummy file and directory."
dateTime:
Write-Host "--- Current Date and Time ---"
$now = Get-Date
Write-Host "DateTime Object: $now"

Write-Host "--- Common Formats (using -Format specifier) ---"
# See Microsoft docs for Get-Date format specifiers (based on .NET formats)
# https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.utility/get-date?view=powershell-7.4#-format
Write-Host "Default Short Date (d): $(Get-Date -Format d)"
Write-Host "Default Long Date (D): $(Get-Date -Format D)"
Write-Host "Default Short Time (t): $(Get-Date -Format t)"
Write-Host "Default Long Time (T): $(Get-Date -Format T)"
Write-Host "Sortable (s): $(Get-Date -Format s)" # ISO 8601 style YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss
Write-Host "YYYY-MM-DD: $(Get-Date -Format 'yyyy-MM-dd')" # Custom format
Write-Host "HH:mm:ss (24hr): $(Get-Date -Format 'HH:mm:ss')"
Write-Host "Readable: $(Get-Date -Format 'dddd, MMMM dd, yyyy hh:mm:ss tt')" # tt = AM/PM

Write-Host "--- Using .NET ToString() method ---"
Write-Host "Using 'g' format: $($now.ToString('g'))" # General short date/time
Write-Host "Using 'F' format: $($now.ToString('F'))" # Full date/time (long date + long time)

Write-Host "--- Components ---"
Write-Host "Year: $($now.Year)"
Write-Host "Month: $($now.Month)"
Write-Host "Day: $($now.Day)"
Write-Host "Hour: $($now.Hour)"
Write-Host "Minute: $($now.Minute)"
Write-Host "Second: $($now.Second)"
Write-Host "DayOfWeek: $($now.DayOfWeek)"

Write-Host "--- Unix Epoch Seconds ---"
# Get UTC time and calculate seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00Z
$epochStart = [DateTime]::new(1970, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, [DateTimeKind]::Utc)
$utcNow = $now.ToUniversalTime()
$epochSeconds = [int]($utcNow - $epochStart).TotalSeconds
Write-Host "Seconds since epoch (UTC): $epochSeconds"

# PowerShell 7+ can use -UFormat %s directly
# Write-Host "Seconds since epoch (PS7+ UFormat): $(Get-Date -UFormat %s)"
